summary: My family traces its lineage back 4500 years to the Yellow Emperor of China. He is a legendary figure whose grandson, Hui, is credited with inventing the bow. The character for our family name, Zhang, is even derived from the bow and concepts like growth or leadership, linking us directly to that ancient heritage.

The Chinese Communist Party took control of mainland China in 1949, radically changing society. They forced landholders to surrender private property, leveling social classes except for those in power. While they made many mistakes, one positive outcome was extending educational rights to women, which directly impacted my maternal grandmother and shaped my family's future.

My mother graduated from Shanghai No. 2 High School, a school notable for also having Yao Ming as an alumnus. This school was an important stepping stone in her educational journey before she went on to university. It represents part of the strong educational foundation my family values deeply.

Yao Ming is a famous figure who also attended Shanghai No. 2 High School, the same school my mother graduated from. Mentioning him highlights the prestige of the school and connects my family's educational background to a well-known personality. Though I never met him, his legacy is part of the school's history.

My mother earned her bachelor's degree from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, which was a significant achievement in her academic career. This university provided her with the education that enabled her to continue to graduate studies at Fudan University. It was a critical part of her path toward higher education in Shanghai.

After completing her bachelor's, my mother pursued her master's degree at Fudan University. This institution furthered her education and helped shape her professional future. Fudan University is one of the top universities in Shanghai, and attending it was a proud moment for our family.

My father was born and raised near Yantai, a coastal city in Shandong province. Growing up there, he was a curious and rebellious kid, but he turned his life around during his senior year of high school. Yantai is where his story of transformation began, leading to his academic success.

Shandong is the province where my father grew up, specifically near the city of Yantai. It shaped his early life and education before he moved on to university. The culture and environment of Shandong played a role in his development as a student and person.

During his senior year of high school, my father took the gaokao, the Chinese college entrance examination, and achieved the highest score in his area. This accomplishment was so notable that a local newspaper published an article about him, calling him the 'number one scholar.' The gaokao was a turning point that opened doors for his future education.

After excelling on the gaokao, my father went on to study at Nanjing University. This university was the next step in his academic journey after high school in Shandong. Attending Nanjing University marked the beginning of his higher education before immigrating to the U.S.

entity: State University of New York at Buffalo
summary: My father came to the United States to further his education at the State University of New York at Buffalo. This move was part of his and my mother's journey to pursue advanced studies abroad. SUNY Buffalo was where he transitioned from Chinese education to American academia.

My mother also immigrated to the U.S. to continue her education, attending Duke University for her graduate studies. Duke was a prestigious institution that helped her build a new life in America. Both my parents' time at Duke and SUNY Buffalo were pivotal in their careers and eventual naturalization.

Much of my family's history was erased during the Cultural Revolution, making it difficult to trace our past beyond my parents' generation. This period was a time of great upheaval in China that affected many families, including mine. Despite this, I maintain a connection to my heritage through language and traditions.
